verbatim: Start with `markbuildsbrands_transcripts` or another small healthy corpus.
action: At S13 kickoff, pin the pilot corpus in the P3.1 experiment manifest: probe markbuildsbrands_transcripts health (parents=1,015, docs=103, 100% summary attribution per PLAN_CRITIQUE section 2) and record corpus_id + collection names resolved via qdrant_writer._col_for_corpus.
who: junior-agent (health probe + manifest); plan of record already names mark
where: docs/PLAN_CRITIQUE_2026-07-13.md S13 row; backend/services/storage/qdrant_writer.py:167 (_col_for_corpus); Mongo parent_chunks/documents for corpus markbuildsbrands_transcripts; new experiment manifest under docs/ or CONTINUITY/
why: a small verified-healthy corpus keeps the thematic pilot cheap and its quality signal interpretable
feasibility_note: Plan of record S13 already resolves the 'or another' clause to mark ('P3.1 thematic pilot (mark)'); PLAN_CRITIQUE section 2 verifies mark at 1,015 parents / 100% heading_path / 100% summary attribution. Caveat: mark has ZERO latent_concepts until S4's paid capture pass — running the pilot before S4/S8 would cluster over pre-capture artifacts (critique C1/C2 pattern). Terminology: 'theme' has NO registry row and no code referent in this sense; backend already uses the word for two unrelated things (services/discourse.py:229,318 discourse themes; chat_orchestrator.py:234 broad-query regex) — register a canonical artifact name (e.g. theme.v0) before build.
required_receipt: Experiment manifest artifact naming corpus_id + a probe transcript (parents=1,015, attribution 100%) with exit-0 assertion, dated at S13 start.

verbatim: Snapshot/scroll existing parent vectors without re-embedding.
action: Write a read-only scroll-snapshot script over the pilot corpus's parent summary points (chunk_type='summary', with_vectors=True) into a versioned snapshot file; assert vector count == parent count and zero calls to the embedder sidecar during the run.
who: junior-agent (script, pattern already exists in scripts_migrate_multitenant.py scroll loop)
where: backend/services/storage/qdrant_writer.py (corpus_<id8>_naive/hrag, summary points keyed parent_id, :888); new backend/scripts snapshot script; snapshot artifact on disk
why: clustering must reuse already-paid embeddings so the pilot's storage/update-cost numbers are honest
feasibility_note: Qdrant scroll with_payload+with_vectors is proven in-repo (scripts_migrate_multitenant.py:73-83). Terminology: 'parent vectors' = Qdrant summary points (parent-level, chunk_type='summary') per registry_leftover 'summary points' — NOT the Tier-0 doc_summary vectors in polymath_doc_summaries (registry_hierarchy 'doc summary' collision); the script must filter chunk_type explicitly or it will mix levels.
required_receipt: Snapshot file with N==1,015 vectors + embedder sidecar request counter unchanged before/after (asserting wrapper, non-zero exit on mismatch).

verbatim: Compare clustering methods and stability across corpus epochs, including soft/non-exclusive membership rather than forcing each parent into one exclusive thematic cluster.
action: Build a clustering comparison harness over the vector snapshot: HDBSCAN (already in-repo pattern) vs k-means vs a soft-membership method (GMM posteriors or HDBSCAN membership_vector), scored on stability (ARI/AMI across two corpus epochs) and membership-entropy; emit per-method report.
who: junior-agent (offline analysis script; no service changes)
where: reuse pattern from backend/services/graph/analytics.py:356-375 (sklearn HDBSCAN + k-means fallback); new offline script + report artifact; input = S13 snapshot
why: soft multi-membership is the core hypothesis separating a thematic layer from the existing single-path tree
feasibility_note: sklearn 1.3 HDBSCAN is already imported in backend (graph/analytics.py:375) so no new dependency class. 'Corpus epochs' should reuse the P1.7 epoch notion (lexicon/cache epoch invalidation, S0) rather than inventing a second epoch counter — registry_alias/corpus_lexicon family owns 'epoch'. Risk: 1,015 parents is small for stability statistics; report must state confidence limits.
required_receipt: Comparison report artifact with per-method stability scores across >=2 epochs and a soft-membership distribution; committed with the chosen method + parameters recorded.

verbatim: Allow one parent, section, document, and concept to belong to multiple evidence-backed themes.
action: Specify the theme-membership schema so one parent/section/document/concept can hold multiple memberships, each with evidence: member_kind + member_id + strength + evidence_ids; pin the ID space per kind before implementation.
who: backend ingestion/librarian (schema); owner reviews the ID-space pinning
where: new theme artifact schema (Mongo collection + optional Qdrant projection); ID sources: parent_chunks.parent_id, summary_tree node ids, documents.doc_id, corpus_lexicon lexicon_id
why: non-exclusive membership is what lets broad questions recruit evidence from several themes at once
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY (multiple registry hits): 'concept' here MUST bind to corpus_lexicon entries (lexicon_id) — registry_latent 'concept contract' orders a split between latent_concepts (parent capture), summary_tree.concepts (tree_concepts), and the lexicon contract; theme membership over raw strings would recreate the aliases-read-by-nobody dead end (critique C3). 'Section' must be pinned to summary_tree node ids, remembering registry_hierarchy's warning that TreeNode.parent_ids are MEMBER parent-chunk ids, not tree ancestry.
required_receipt: Schema doc + validator (P0.8 style) showing a worked example of one parent and one lexicon_id belonging to two themes with evidence_ids; validator test exit 0.

verbatim: Allow one query and each of its sub-queries to activate multiple themes within and across selected corpora.
action: Implement query-time theme activation returning a SET of themes per query and per sub-query, filtered to the user-selected corpus boundary; assert the original-query lane is untouched by theme expansion.
where: retrieval planning seam (backend/services/retriever/, alongside planned-pool fusion); theme index from L1109; selected_corpus_ids filter
why: multi-theme activation is the router behavior that should lift broad/cross-document recall
feasibility_note: Two standing invariants bind this: cross-corpus tests preserve the exact user-selected corpus boundary, and the original query remains a protected retrieval lane (checklist Non-Negotiable Invariants). registry_leftover 'corpus boundary' canonical = selected_corpus_ids filter. Sub-queries should reuse the existing decomposer lane rather than a second decomposition path.
required_receipt: Three-tier probe transcript showing >=2 activated themes on a broad query, zero out-of-boundary corpus_ids in candidates, and an unchanged original-query lane (diagnostics assertion, non-zero exit on violation).

verbatim: Generate evidence-backed summaries for themes with member parent IDs, membership strength, and source provenance.
action: Generate theme summaries as a new provenance-stamped artifact class: each row carries member parent IDs, membership strength, source provenance, plus summary_model/engine/schema_version/validation status per the P0.1 discipline; no empty-model placeholders.
who: backend ingestion (Ghost A-style generation path) + junior-agent verify script
where: new theme summary store (Mongo); generation via the existing summary provider stack; validators in backend/services/storage/schema_validators.py
why: evidence-backed theme summaries are what make themes usable for routing without hallucinated abstraction
feasibility_note: This creates a PAID artifact class — standing rule 1 (capture-before-rebuild) applies: before generating, list open capture hooks for the summary seam; at S13 the S1/S4 temporal+latent capture should already be landed, so the seam is clean. Invariant: 'Summary...artifacts carry explicit model/engine, schema version, evidence, and validation status' is verbatim in the ledger. Theme count is small (tens), so cost is trivial vs S4.
required_receipt: Verify script (P0.1-style) over the theme summary store: 0 empty-model rows, 100% rows with member parent IDs + strengths + evidence provenance; exit 0.

verbatim: Index themes with explicit abstraction level, version, corpus ownership, and soft member bindings.
action: Design theme index storage (abstraction level, version, corpus ownership, soft member bindings) AGAINST the P3.3 target collection layout, not the legacy per-corpus layout; if a Qdrant projection is added, corpus_id rides the payload with a keyword index and the theme's first consumer (the router, L1113) ships in the same phase.
who: backend storage + retriever; owner ratifies the storage placement
where: new Mongo theme collection + optional Qdrant projection; layout decision coupled to P3.3 (qdrant_writer._col_for_corpus is today's only legal resolver per registry_leftover 'qdrant collections')
why: a versioned, corpus-owned theme index is what makes themes re-buildable and auditable per epoch
feasibility_note: Standing rule 2 (no dark fields) binds: the theme index must ship with its named consumer or be marked dark data. Standing rule 4 (one migration per seam): P3.1 storage and P3.3 consolidation both touch Qdrant collection layout in the same S13 window — co-schedule so the theme index is not created in a layout P3.3 immediately migrates away from.
required_receipt: Schema + storage-decision note referencing the P3.3 design; probe showing themes queryable by (corpus_id, version, abstraction level) with soft bindings present.

verbatim: Fuse evidence across activated themes; never use a single thematic winner as a hard gate over all downstream retrieval.
action: Implement theme-evidence fusion as an additional candidate lane merged by the existing planned-pool fusion, with an explicit test that a single top theme CANNOT zero out other lanes (no hard gate); themes contribute candidates and priors only.
where: backend/services/retriever/ planned-pool fusion seam (registry_retrieval canonical: planned_pool_fusion / fuse_planned_pools); diagnostics
why: fusion-not-gating preserves recall for queries whose answer spans or defies the theme partition
feasibility_note: Registry_retrieval 'fusion' is DISTINCT-verdict: four fusion mechanisms exist (planned_pool_fusion, qdrant_rrf, rank_fusion_fallback, multi_score_fusion) — this claim means planned_pool_fusion; naming it loosely risks wiring themes into the wrong layer. Also aligned with the production-redesign direction (cross-encoder = sole scoring authority): themes route, the CE scores.
required_receipt: Unit/e2e test: with theme A dominant, evidence from non-A lanes still reaches the final packet on a crafted query; assertion test exit 0.

verbatim: Use themes as routers first; allow synthesis use only with hydrated evidence.
action: Enforce the router-first rule in code the same way Tier-0 does for doc cards: theme summaries route but are NEVER emitted as evidence; synthesis may quote theme content only after parent hydration of member evidence (parent_hydration).
where: mirror backend/services/retriever/tier0.py docstring contract ('routing card, NEVER evidence'); hydration via hydrate_chunks (registry_retrieval 'hydration' canonical parent_hydration)
why: prevents un-grounded thematic abstraction from leaking into answers as fake citations
feasibility_note: Direct precedent exists: registry_hierarchy 'doc summary' row records the tier0.py card/evidence split — reuse that enforcement pattern rather than inventing a parallel one. Consistent with the owner-architecture rule that assembly is deterministic and hydration is budget-ruled.
required_receipt: Test asserting a theme summary id never appears in the final evidence packet's source chunks while its member parents (hydrated) do; exit 0.

verbatim: Diagnose themes activated, themes covered, themes unsupported, and contributions by activated themes to final evidence.
action: Add theme diagnostics to the retrieval trace record: themes_activated, themes_covered, themes_unsupported, and per-theme contribution counts to the final evidence packet; ride the existing three-tier eval record contract.
where: retrieval diagnostics contract (backend/services/retriever/three_tier_eval.py record contract; chat trace metadata on the retrieval DONE event per live-chat-probe recipe)
why: without activation/coverage diagnostics the acceptance lines below are unmeasurable
feasibility_note: The S14 regression matrix already commits to an 8-field record contract per row — theme fields must be added to the SAME record contract, not a side channel, or S14 cannot audit the pilot. Diagnostics-first matches the silent-fallback-accounting standing feedback (every mechanism counts and surfaces its rate).
required_receipt: Authed /api/chat SSE probe transcript showing all four theme diagnostic fields populated on a broad query; asserting wrapper exit 0.

verbatim: Evaluate broad recall, precision, storage, update cost, and latency.
action: Run the pilot evaluation on the P1.1 held-out suite: broad recall, precision, storage bytes for the theme layer, update cost per epoch (wall + paid tokens), and p50/p95 latency delta vs the S8 baseline; publish a before/after artifact.
who: junior-agent (eval harness run); backend provides diagnostics
where: heldout suite + scorer (registry_eval canonical 'heldout eval', run_heldout_eval.score, scorer_version-stamped); baselines under docs/baselines; theme store for storage numbers
why: the pilot is an Experiment-tagged item — no adoption without before/after numbers by its own status legend
feasibility_note: Blocked-in-order, not blocked-in-fact: the S8 card-rebuild A/B baseline (held-out 56 vs 2026-07-13 baseline) must exist first, which the S-order guarantees. Hypothesis-promotion rule (standing feedback): numeric targets must be set IN ADVANCE of this run, which is what L1122's 'materially' needs (see that row).
required_receipt: Eval artifact (scorer_version-stamped) with all five metric families vs the S8 baseline, plus the pre-registered target sheet it is scored against.

verbatim: Multiple relevant themes can survive routing and final evidence selection.
action: Add an acceptance test to the heldout harness: on designated broad queries, >=2 distinct themes appear in themes_activated AND both contribute chunks surviving into the final evidence packet (via the L1115 diagnostics).
where: heldout suite queries + three_tier_eval record contract; theme diagnostics fields
why: proves soft routing survives ranking/reservation instead of collapsing to a single thematic winner
feasibility_note: Directly testable once L1115 diagnostics exist; needs 'multiple relevant' operationalized as a labeled expectation on specific heldout rows (the suite supports per-row expected shapes). Depends on L1111's no-hard-gate fusion being real.
required_receipt: Acceptance test run: named heldout query ids each showing >=2 themes with nonzero final-packet contribution; exit 0.

verbatim: Themes materially improve broad/cross-document questions.
action: OWNER: set the numeric 'materially improves' threshold in advance (e.g. +X points broad-recall / cross-document coverage on the heldout broad-question subset at <=Y ms p95 cost), then junior-agent scores the pilot against it.
where: pre-registered target sheet alongside docs/baselines; heldout broad/cross-document query subset
why: an un-numbered 'materially' invites post-hoc rationalization of a marginal pilot
feasibility_note: The hypothesis-promotion standing feedback is explicit: promote only against numeric targets set in advance. The eval machinery (P1.1 suite, scorer_version) exists to score it; only the number is missing, and only the owner can set it because it trades latency/storage against recall.
required_receipt: Signed pre-registered threshold in the target sheet BEFORE the eval run, then the eval artifact meeting it (or the pilot is recorded as not adopted).

verbatim: Focused questions do not lose source precision.
action: Add a no-regress gate: focused/single-source heldout questions must keep source precision (correct-document rate, per-doc citation accuracy) within the agreed epsilon of the S8 baseline with themes enabled.
where: heldout focused-question subset; eval_metrics precision fields; S8 baseline artifact
why: protects the existing focused-retrieval quality from being taxed to pay for breadth
feasibility_note: This is the standard before/after no-regress shape the P1.1 standing gate already commits to; the theme layer being router-only (L1113) plus no-hard-gate fusion (L1111) is the mechanism that should make it pass. Epsilon should ride the same pre-registered target sheet as L1122.
required_receipt: Eval artifact: focused-subset precision delta vs S8 baseline within epsilon; asserting comparison script exit 0.

verbatim: Only after success may the architecture claim a thematic RAPTOR layer.
action: Record in the ledger that the 'thematic RAPTOR layer' name may be claimed only after L1121-L1123 pass; until then keep calling the existing tree summary_tree in all docs and code comments.
where: docs/RAPTOR_RAG_IMPLEMENTATION_CHECKLIST.md P3.1 acceptance; docs/RAPTOR_RAG_CRITIQUE_AND_ROADMAP.md:39
why: prevents the architecture from re-claiming a name the current system explicitly does not earn
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY (registry_hierarchy 'RAPTOR tree' row, failure mode b): TWO concepts share the name RAPTOR — the existing heading-grouped summary_tree (a misnomer: 'closer to a doc-summary index + auto-merging than to RAPTOR', no clustering) and this to-be-built thematic layer. A grep-driven implementer could 'extend RAPTOR' in the wrong place. This acceptance line is the ledger's own guard against that collision; treat it as a naming gate, not work.
required_receipt: Ledger diff showing the acceptance box checked only in the same commit as (or after) the L1121-L1123 green artifacts.

verbatim: Select only evidence-strong entities/relations spanning multiple corpora.
action: Build a Neo4j selection query for entities/relations whose evidence spans >=2 corpora (Entity.corpus_ids[] cardinality >=2) with per-side typed-evidence minimums; emit a candidate list with support counts per corpus.
who: junior-agent (graph query + report); backend graph service for productionizing
where: Neo4j typed edges + Entity.corpus_ids[] (corpus isolation = properties + corpus_ids[], never ID-prefixing); ghost_b_extractions.relations as durable evidence source
why: bridges are only trustworthy when both corpus sides independently evidence the same entity/relation
feasibility_note: Substrate exists: registry_extraction 'relations' maps the full chain (RelationItem -> ghost_b_extractions.relations -> Neo4j edge -> lexicon factual_relations). Gotchas from memory apply: canonical entity_id uses HYPHENS (neo4j_writer.entity_id_from_name) and indexed-property matching (never function-wrap in WHERE) or the selection query will silently miss or time out. Sequencing: richer after S11's pair re-extraction; S13 order already ensures that.
required_receipt: Candidate-list artifact: N entities/relations each with >=2 corpus_ids and per-side evidence counts; spot-checked sample with real evidence_ids resolving to chunks.

verbatim: Exclude generic/high-degree `RELATES_TO` hubs without typed support.
action: Add a hub-exclusion filter to the selection: drop entities whose cross-corpus connectivity is carried only by RELATES_TO (untyped family) or whose graph degree exceeds a percentile threshold without at least one typed relation per side.
where: Neo4j degree query over the RELATION_FAMILY_MAP typed-vs-RELATES_TO split; selection pipeline from L1128
why: high-degree generic hubs create false bridges that route users to unrelated corpora
feasibility_note: Well-grounded: the Ghost-B predicate-floor memory establishes ~50% related_to as the floor for clean entities — meaning RELATES_TO mass is expected and MUST be discounted, not treated as signal. Typed support is queryable because the family map already types edges (registry_extraction).
required_receipt: Before/after filter report: hub count removed, degree distribution, and zero surviving bridges whose only cross-corpus support is RELATES_TO.

verbatim: Store bridge concepts, source corpora, documents, predicates, confidence, and evidence IDs.
action: Define and validate the bridge-card row schema {bridge concept id, source corpora, doc_ids, predicates, confidence, evidence_ids} as a new versioned Mongo artifact, with a P0.8-style validator; the evidence predicate must be computed by the SAME module as shelf_engine's bridge role.
where: new Mongo collection (bridge cards); shared predicate extracted from backend/services/librarian/shelf_engine.py (_role_bridge / principle_bridge criteria); schema_validators.py
why: a materialized, evidence-carrying bridge row is what makes cross-corpus routing auditable
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY + duplicate-implementation risk (registry_librarian 'bridge' row, FAILURE MODE b): P3.2 bridge cards and transfer_edge both materialize the SAME join _role_bridge computes at query time (shared principle/mechanism + different central subjects + two-sided evidence). Build ONE bridge-evidence predicate module consumed by shelf_engine, bridge cards, and transfer_edge, or thresholds will drift into three definitions of 'bridge'. Also note 'bridge' separately names the answerability-gate LLM-bridging behavior — unrelated mechanism, same word.
required_receipt: Schema validator test exit 0 + a sample bridge card whose evidence_ids hydrate to real chunks on BOTH corpora; grep proof that shelf_engine and the card writer import the same predicate module.

verbatim: Use bridge cards for shelf/document routing, not unsupported final claims.
action: Wire bridge cards into shelf/document routing only (P1.4 shelf routing seam): cards may nominate corpora/documents as candidates but never appear as final-claim evidence; enforce with the same router-not-evidence pattern as Tier-0 and L1113.
where: P1.4 shelf routing seam + shelf_engine document seats; final evidence packet assembly (must exclude card content)
why: keeps cross-corpus suggestions as navigation, never as unsupported synthesized claims
feasibility_note: Standing invariant match: 'only deterministic evidence-backed policy may grant final document seats' — bridge cards are deterministic and evidence-backed, so they legally feed seat nomination. Dependency: P1.4 shelf routing is 0/8 boxes today (PLAN_CRITIQUE table) and lands S9-ish via S8/S9 work; by S13 the routing seam exists. Standing rule 2: the card's first consumer (this router) ships in the same phase as the card.
required_receipt: e2e probe: a cross-corpus query shows bridge-card-nominated documents in routing diagnostics while zero bridge-card text appears in final evidence; asserting probe exit 0.

verbatim: Evaluate non-obvious cross-domain recall and false-bridge rate.
action: Extend the heldout suite with cross-domain rows (non-obvious two-corpus questions) plus labeled negative pairs; measure cross-domain recall and false-bridge rate (bridges asserted where the negative label says none exists) with pre-registered thresholds.
who: junior-agent (suite extension + scored run); owner sets thresholds
where: heldout suite (P1.1) + scorer; bridge-card store for assertions
why: false bridges are the failure mode that turns a knowledge tool into a confabulation engine
feasibility_note: The negative-controls machinery exists conceptually in the suite (registry_eval 'negative controls' — keep 'forbidden-vocabulary control' distinct); false-bridge labeling is new manual work on the PoC pair, tractable at pair scale. Numeric thresholds must precede the run (hypothesis-promotion rule).
required_receipt: Eval artifact: cross-domain recall + false-bridge rate vs pre-registered thresholds; negative rows fail closed (0 bridges asserted).

verbatim: Materialize deterministic `transfer_edge` rows when documents with meaningfully different central subjects share a specific validated principle or mechanism.
action: Implement the deterministic transfer_edge writer: emit a row only when two documents with different central_subjects (librarian_cards.central_subjects disjointness test) share a validated principle/mechanism (lexicon-ID'd), using the shared bridge-evidence predicate from L1130.
where: new transfer_edge store (Mongo or Neo4j edge class); inputs: librarian_cards.central_subjects, corpus_lexicon entries (principle/mechanism IDs), two-sided evidence_ids
why: transfer edges are the durable form of 'this principle transfers across domains' — the product's core promise
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY: 'central subjects' is canonical librarian_cards.central_subjects (registry_latent) — and that row's guard applies: central_subjects deliberately EXCLUDES latent_concepts (those live in candidate_latent_subjects and can corroborate but never solely qualify); the disjointness test must run on central_subjects proper. 'Principle/mechanism ID' presumes P2.1's lexicon contract (mechanisms currently live as parent_chunks fields per CONCEPT_SOURCE_FIELDS; the lexicon-ID form arrives via S5/P2.1) — S13 ordering satisfies this, earlier attempts would be blocked. registry_librarian flags transfer_edge as PLAN-ONLY sharing _role_bridge's inputs: reuse the one predicate module.
required_receipt: Writer test: crafted fixture pair produces exactly one transfer_edge with both-side evidence; a same-subject pair produces zero; exit 0.

verbatim: Store endpoint document/concept IDs, principle/mechanism ID, evidence IDs on both sides, support counts, specificity, temporal scope, and policy version.
action: Define the transfer_edge row contract with all listed fields (endpoint doc/concept IDs, principle/mechanism lexicon ID, evidence IDs both sides, support counts, specificity, temporal scope, policy version) and a P0.8-style validator; policy_version bumps with any threshold change in the shared bridge predicate.
where: transfer_edge store schema; schema_validators.py; temporal scope fields from the T-MAIN capability seam (S12)
why: versioned, evidence-complete rows are what make transfer edges re-derivable and prunable when policy changes
feasibility_note: Temporal scope is only meaningful after S12's T-MAIN phases populate temporal fields (temporal_class/temporal_captures are 0 rows everywhere today, PLAN_CRITIQUE section 2) — S13 order makes this legal; an earlier build would store empty temporal scopes and violate the no-dark-fields rule. 'Specificity' should reuse the P2.1 DF/specificity field family rather than a new ad-hoc score (registry_facet 'DF' canonical parent_df).
required_receipt: Validator test exit 0 over fixture rows including a temporal-scoped edge; rejection of a row missing either side's evidence_ids.

verbatim: Do not create transfer edges from embedding proximity alone.
action: Enforce as a code-level guard: the transfer_edge writer path has no embedding-similarity input; add a negative test that a high-cosine but evidence-free document pair yields zero edges. Keep as a standing gate in review.
where: transfer_edge writer (L1134); test fixture with embedding-close, evidence-disjoint docs
why: embedding proximity alone manufactures plausible-but-false domain transfers
feasibility_note: Mirrors the ledger's core invariant (embeddings may generate candidates; only deterministic evidence-backed policy grants seats/edges). Cheap to enforce because the L1134 design is already deterministic — the test pins it against future 'just add a similarity fallback' drift, the exact silent-fallback failure the standing feedback bans.
required_receipt: Negative test in CI: cosine-similar/evidence-free pair -> 0 transfer_edges, exit 0; grep showing no embedding call in the writer module.

verbatim: Document why hrag, naive, graph, schemas, sparse, and payload contracts differ today.
action: Write the layout archaeology doc: for each of corpus_<id8>_{naive,hrag,graph,schemas} plus sparse-vector configs and per-kind payload contracts, state why it exists, who reads it (grep call sites), and what would break if merged; ground in qdrant_writer.py and live collection listing.
where: backend/services/storage/qdrant_writer.py:125-176 (_col_for_corpus + module docstring); config.py:645; live GET /collections (20 collections today); output doc under docs/
why: consolidation decisions made without knowing why the shapes diverged will re-learn it via outages
feasibility_note: Purely read-only and cheap; could run any time but earns nothing before the S13 experiment consumes it. TERMINOLOGY: registry_leftover 'qdrant collections' canonical = per-corpus corpus_<id8>_{naive,hrag,graph,schemas} + universal polymath_doc_summaries; note 'sparse' in the claim is a NAMED SPARSE VECTOR inside collections (SparseVectorParams modifier=IDF), not a fifth collection kind — the doc should correct that wording.
required_receipt: Committed doc mapping every live collection kind to purpose + reader call sites; reviewed against the live /collections listing.

verbatim: Treat the existing `polymath_children` migration as unfinished: the shared collection currently has only a few points and `QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS` has no active retrieval cutover path.
action: Adopt scripts_migrate_multitenant.py (B4 groundwork, flag-gated OFF) as the declared starting point of the P3.3 migration; ledger-mark the polymath_children state as 'stub (3 points), no retrieval consumer' and fold its completion into the L1156-L1158 design/cutover work.
where: backend/scripts_migrate_multitenant.py; config.py:228 QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS (default False); Qdrant polymath_children
why: an acknowledged-unfinished migration stub left undocumented becomes a data-integrity trap later
feasibility_note: Fact behind the framing verified live today (see L1148F). The script header itself says retrieval keeps per-corpus collections 'until QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS flips (owner call)' — and grep shows ZERO consumers of that flag in backend/services/, so flipping it today would change nothing: the cutover path genuinely does not exist and must be BUILT, not enabled. This claim is correctly framed; it is a scoping instruction for L1156-L1158.
required_receipt: Ledger/doc note recording the stub state + a linked design task; later closed by L1158's cutover receipt.

verbatim: The shared collection currently has only a few points and `QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS` has no active retrieval cutover path.
action: Re-check the two facts: `curl -s http://localhost:6333/collections/polymath_children | jq .result.points_count` (expect single-digit; probed 2026-07-13 by this critic: 3) and `grep -rn QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS backend/services/` (expect zero hits = no retrieval consumer; only config.py:228 defines it).
where: Qdrant polymath_children; backend/config.py:228; backend/services/ (grep)
why: grounds the consolidation scope: the 'shared collection' is a 3-point stub, not a half-migrated store
feasibility_note: VERIFIED live during this critique: points_count=3 on polymath_children, flag default False, zero consumers in services/. TERMINOLOGY: 'the shared collection' is ambiguous — polymath_doc_summaries is ALSO shared and IS live (Tier-0 routing); only polymath_children is the stub. Any consolidation sentence must name which shared collection it means (registry_hierarchy 'doc summary' row).
required_receipt: Probe transcript: points_count=3 (or current) + empty grep over services/, dated.

verbatim: Make `corpus_id` a Qdrant tenant index in any shared collection because every live search is corpus-scoped; create it before HNSW construction.
action: Recreate the shared children collection with corpus_id declared as a true Qdrant tenant index (KeywordIndexParams(is_tenant=True)) BEFORE bulk migration, and patch scripts_migrate_multitenant.py._ensure which today creates only plain KEYWORD payload indexes; the 3-point stub makes drop/recreate free right now.
where: backend/scripts_migrate_multitenant.py:47-53 (_ensure index loop); Qdrant polymath_children (drop/recreate)
why: tenant-aware HNSW keeps per-corpus search fast inside a shared collection — the whole point of consolidation
feasibility_note: GAP FOUND: grep is_tenant over backend = zero hits; the existing migration script's corpus_id index is a plain keyword index, NOT a tenant index, and polymath_children already exists with HNSW config — so as written the checklist requirement ('create it before HNSW construction') is currently violated by the very script that would run the migration. Because the collection holds only 3 points, recreating it correctly is a one-minute operation TODAY; after a real migration it becomes a full rebuild. Consider pulling just the recreate+patch forward of S13 as cheap insurance.
required_receipt: GET /collections/polymath_children payload_schema showing corpus_id with is_tenant=true, created while points_count is still ~0; script diff.

verbatim: Every live search is corpus-scoped.
action: Re-verify 'every live search is corpus-scoped': grep all Qdrant search entry points (qdrant_writer.py search fns :298,1264,1314,1603 + retriever call sites) and confirm each either resolves a per-corpus collection via _col_for_corpus or filters corpus_id on a shared collection (retriever/__init__.py:702 documents the doc-summaries payload filter).
where: backend/services/storage/qdrant_writer.py; backend/services/retriever/__init__.py:702,3689-3691 (stale-corpus filtering)
why: the tenant-index justification stands or falls on there being no corpus-unscoped search path
feasibility_note: Structurally true today by construction: hot paths resolve collections per-corpus (_col_for_corpus is 'the only legal resolver', registry_leftover), and the one live shared collection (polymath_doc_summaries) is corpus_id-filtered with stale-corpus dropping at :3689. The audit is a one-hour grep pass; the only way the fact breaks is a future cross-corpus discovery feature, which would then need the tenant index even more.
required_receipt: Audit note listing every search call site with its scoping mechanism; zero unscoped sites.

verbatim: Represent Fast/Hybrid/Graph eligibility as versioned point payload or named-vector policy so one child vector is not stored three times merely to expose three retrieval routes.
action: Design route eligibility as data, not duplication: one child point per vector with a versioned payload field (e.g. routes: [fast, hybrid, graph] or per-route named vectors) replacing the naive/hrag/graph collection fan-out; prototype on the cloned pilot corpus and measure storage delta.
where: qdrant_writer upsert_children + collection layout; RetrievalTier consumers (models/_schemas_legacy.py:1068)
why: storing one child vector three times to expose three routes is pure write/RAM amplification
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY (registry_retrieval 'tier', OVERLAPPING): Fast/Hybrid/Graph here means RetrievalTier (qdrant_only|qdrant_mongo|qdrant_mongo_graph) — 'tier' also names source_tier provenance strings, Tier-0 routing, and tier_chunker's ingestion Tiers A/B/C; the design doc must use RetrievalTier explicitly. Registry_hierarchy 'child chunk' confirms children live in BOTH naive and hrag point sets today, validating the duplication premise. Interacts with L1151's tenant index — one design, one migration (standing rule 4).
required_receipt: Design doc + pilot-corpus prototype showing one point serving all three RetrievalTier routes with per-route parity vs the legacy layout on a fixed query set.

verbatim: Design a versioned single/multi-collection target with rollback.
action: Produce the versioned target-layout design (single vs multi-collection, schema_version stamped, explicit rollback = keep per-corpus sources intact and flip QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS-successor flag back); owner signs the design before any bulk write.
where: design doc under docs/; config flag lifecycle (config.py:228 successor); scripts_migrate_multitenant.py as the additive-reversible pattern
why: a migration without a written rollback is a bet, not an experiment
feasibility_note: The additive+reversible pattern is already established by the B4 script (sources untouched, deterministic point ids, idempotent re-runs) — the design extends it rather than inventing it. Migration-tagged per the status legend: durable artifacts change, so the design must precede writes. Owner involvement is genuine (layout is a long-lived architectural commitment).
required_receipt: Signed design doc naming target layout, version stamp, cutover flag, and a tested rollback procedure (executed once on the pilot clone).

verbatim: Benchmark filtering, graph isolation, write amplification, RAM, and recall.
action: Run the benchmark matrix on the pilot clone: filtered-search latency (tenant index on/off), graph-route isolation, write amplification (bytes written per logical child), RAM per collection, and recall parity vs the legacy layout at fixed queries.
where: cloned pilot corpus collections (legacy vs target layout); Qdrant telemetry endpoints; benchmark artifact under docs/baselines
why: consolidation is justified only by measured wins, not by aesthetic preference for fewer collections
feasibility_note: All five axes are measurable with existing tooling (Qdrant /collections telemetry, docker stats, fixed heldout query subset for recall). Must run AFTER L1151's tenant-index recreate or the filtering numbers will be measured against the wrong index type and the experiment re-bought.
required_receipt: Benchmark artifact with all five metric families, legacy vs target, same hardware, dated; stored beside the S8 baseline for comparison.

verbatim: Migrate one small corpus through a resumable dual-write/cutover.
action: Execute the small-corpus migration end-to-end: resumable dual-write (idempotent deterministic point ids), point/count + spot-vector parity check, BUILD the retrieval cutover path (flag consumer in the retriever — it does not exist today), flip for the pilot corpus only, and verify three-tier parity live.
who: backend storage + retriever; junior-agent parity checks
where: scripts_migrate_multitenant.py (extend); NEW flag consumer in backend/services/retriever/ + qdrant_writer collection resolution; pilot = UGO_CORPUS (canary, 203 parents) or mark
why: a rehearsed, reversible small-corpus cutover is the only safe proof of the consolidation design
feasibility_note: Feasible but the riskiest P3.3 item: the cutover consumer is NEW retrieval-path code (grep confirms zero QDRANT_SHARED_COLLECTIONS readers), touching the hottest seam in the product. Mitigations already in-pattern: additive dual-write, per-corpus flag scope, UGO as the designated canary (plan ground rules), and the assert-before-commit standing feedback (self-asserting e2e, not log-greps). Do not schedule against S11's extraction burst (shared Qdrant I/O).
required_receipt: Parity artifact (source vs shared: exact point counts + sampled vector/payload equality) AND a three-tier e2e probe on the flipped pilot corpus, both exit 0; rollback rehearsal transcript.

verbatim: Produce and approve an ownership manifest, then remove empty/deleted/orphan collections and stale shared routing cards through the durable cleanup path.
action: Generate the ownership manifest (every Qdrant collection -> owning corpus/feature/reader call sites, from L1146's doc + live listing), obtain owner approval, then route deletions of empty/orphan collections and stale shared routing cards through the P0.6 durable cleanup machinery — never ad-hoc drops.
who: junior-agent (manifest); owner (approval); backend P0.6 cleanup path (execution)
where: live /collections listing (20 today); P0.6 cleanup leases/reconciliation; polymath_doc_summaries stale routing cards
why: orphan collections and stale routing cards silently corrupt Tier-0 routing and inflate RAM
feasibility_note: Two gates: (1) manifest approval is a genuine owner decision (deletion is destructive); (2) the 'durable cleanup path' is P0.6 machinery whose own acceptance is incomplete (lease test matrix PARTIAL: restart-before-expiry, purge-beyond-lease; scheduled reconciliation open — PLAN_CRITIQUE table). Deleting through a cleanup path whose lease semantics are unproven is the exact trap the path exists to prevent; close the P0.6 remainders first or scope this to reversible archival.
required_receipt: Approved manifest artifact + cleanup-path execution log showing each removal leased, reconciled, and verified absent; zero direct drop calls outside the path.

verbatim: Do not migrate large corpora until exact point/count parity is proven.
action: Keep as the migration gate: no corpus beyond the pilot moves until L1158's parity artifact shows EXACT point/count parity; polymath_v2 additionally stays untouched under the heavy-ops freeze regardless of parity.
who: owner-enforced gate; junior-agent parity checks per corpus
where: parity harness from L1158; plan ground rule 'polymath_v2 frozen for heavy ops' (PLAN_CRITIQUE header)
why: a large-corpus migration with silent point loss is an unrecoverable retrieval regression
feasibility_note: Standing guardrail, not work; doubly binding for v2 (130,503 parents) which the plan freezes independently. Consistent with the no-cached-plan-execution and assert-before-commit standing feedbacks: parity is a fresh per-corpus receipt, never assumed from the pilot's success.
required_receipt: Per-corpus parity artifact (exact counts + sampled equality, exit 0) attached to every migration beyond the pilot; absence of any v2 migration entry while the freeze stands.

verbatim: Measure current vector RAM/disk by collection and representation: Qdrant volume about 26 GiB, container 6.63/8 GiB, active vectors/HNSW in memory, payload on disk, no quantization.
action: Re-verify the [x] receipt and complete it to spec: the item requires measurement 'by collection and representation' but the receipt is aggregate-only. Run `docker stats --no-stream <qdrant>` + `du -sh` on the Qdrant volume + per-collection GET /collections/<name> (vectors count, segment sizes, on_disk_payload) and publish the per-collection breakdown.
where: Qdrant container + volume; /collections telemetry per collection (20 collections); baseline artifact under docs/baselines
why: quantization targeting needs to know WHICH collections hold the RAM, not just the total
feasibility_note: The aggregate measurement exists (ledger receipt: ~26 GiB volume, 6.63/8 GiB container) and matches the config observed live (on_disk_payload=true on polymath_children; no quantization config anywhere in backend — grep clean). But the box's own wording ('by collection and representation') is only partially satisfied: no per-collection breakdown artifact is cited, and the numbers predate the S4/S11 data passes so they will drift. Treat the [x] as done for the aggregate, re-measure per-collection at S13 start.
required_receipt: Dated per-collection table (points, vector RAM estimate, disk, quantization=none) + container/volume totals; stored as the P3.4 pre-experiment baseline.

verbatim: Qdrant volume about 26 GiB, container 6.63/8 GiB, active vectors/HNSW in memory, payload on disk, no quantization.
action: Re-check the numbers before the experiment: `docker system df -v | grep qdrant` (volume ~26 GiB), `docker stats --no-stream` (container mem vs 8 GiB limit), and confirm no quantization_config on any collection via GET /collections/<name>.
where: Docker host (Mac); Qdrant HTTP API :6333
why: stale RAM/disk figures would mis-size the quantization win and the adoption thresholds
feasibility_note: Fact is plausible and partially corroborated live today (polymath_children config shows on_disk_payload=true and no quantization_config; hnsw defaults m=16/ef_construct=100). The 26 GiB / 6.63-of-8 GiB figures are ledger-receipt numbers from ~2026-07-13 and WILL drift after S4 (pair summary regen) and S11 (re-extraction) write passes — re-measure at S13 start rather than trusting the receipt.
required_receipt: Fresh probe transcript with the three numbers, dated at S13 start.

verbatim: Apply scalar quantization to a cloned small corpus.
action: Clone the pilot corpus's collections (Qdrant snapshot or scroll-copy, the scripts_migrate_multitenant.py pattern) into *_quantexp collections and apply ScalarQuantization(int8) config; production collections untouched.
where: new corpus_<id8>_*_quantexp collections; Qdrant collection create with quantization_config; source = mark or UGO collections
why: clone-first keeps the experiment reversible and the production RAM profile stable
feasibility_note: No quantization code exists in backend today (grep: only an unrelated test string), so this is all-new but small: Qdrant natively supports scalar int8 with always_ram + rescoring. The plan of record explicitly tags P3.4 'isolated experiments' (S13) and the ledger tags it Experiment = no production adoption without before/after. Clone cost is trivial at pilot scale (~1-10k points).
required_receipt: GET on the clone showing quantization_config scalar int8 present, point count == source, production collections' configs unchanged.

verbatim: Evaluate scalar `int8` with quantized vectors in RAM, original vectors on disk, rescoring enabled, and multiple `hnsw_ef` values against exact-search ground truth.
action: Build the quantization eval harness: exact-search ground truth (search with exact=true), then int8 with quantized-in-RAM + originals-on-disk + rescore=true, sweeping hnsw_ef over >=3 values; score recall overlap vs ground truth per ef value.
where: *_quantexp clone; Qdrant search params (hnsw_ef, quantization.rescore, exact); harness script + artifact
why: ef/rescore sweeps are where quantized recall is won or lost — a single operating point proves nothing
feasibility_note: All required knobs are native Qdrant search parameters; none are used in backend today (grep hnsw_ef = zero hits), so the harness is standalone and cannot disturb production paths. Ground-truth via exact=true on the same clone removes ANN noise from the comparison.
required_receipt: Harness artifact: recall-vs-ground-truth table across ef values with/without rescore, reproducible via a single script invocation, exit 0.

verbatim: Measure the APFS bind-mount I/O cost of rescoring before adopting an on-disk-original design.
action: First confirm how the Qdrant volume is actually mounted (`docker inspect <qdrant> | jq .[].Mounts` — named volume vs APFS bind-mount), then measure rescoring I/O cost: p50/p95 search latency with rescore on/off under cold and warm page cache on that mount type.
where: Docker mount config on the Mac host; *_quantexp clone with on-disk originals
why: on-disk-original designs live or die on random-read latency of the actual storage path, not a datasheet
feasibility_note: Sound and Mac-specific (APFS + Docker Desktop file-sharing overhead is a real, known tax). One wording check: the claim ASSUMES a bind-mount; if the volume is a named Docker volume inside the Linux VM the I/O profile differs materially — the mount-type check must come first or the experiment measures the wrong substrate.
required_receipt: Artifact: mount type + rescore-on/off latency table (cold/warm); a stated GO/NO-GO on the on-disk-original design derived from it.

verbatim: Evaluate recall@85, nDCG, routed-document coverage, final evidence coverage, p50/p95, and RAM; Qdrant latency alone is not the quality gate.
action: Score the quantization experiment on retrieval-quality metrics end-to-end: define 'recall@85' precisely in the eval spec BEFORE running (recall at the production candidate depth vs exact ground truth — pin the number), plus nDCG, routed-document coverage, final-evidence coverage, p50/p95, RAM; run through the real retriever, not raw Qdrant search.
where: heldout suite + three_tier_eval on the clone; eval_metrics library; RAM from docker stats
why: Qdrant-local latency can improve while downstream evidence coverage silently degrades — the gate must be end-to-end
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY: 'recall@85' has no registry row and is nonstandard — ambiguous between recall@k=85 and an 85%-recall target; the eval spec must define it (registry_eval: metrics live in eval_metrics, artifacts stamp scorer_version). The item's own caveat ('Qdrant latency alone is not the quality gate') matches the plan's end-to-end discipline; routed-document and final-evidence coverage require the L1115-style diagnostics contract, all present by S13.
required_receipt: scorer_version-stamped eval artifact with all six metric families, quantized vs unquantized clone, plus the pre-registered metric definitions sheet.

verbatim: Adopt only if quality loss and latency meet agreed thresholds.
action: OWNER: pre-register the adoption thresholds (max recall/nDCG loss, max p95 delta, min RAM saving) before the L1174 run; adoption then = flipping quantization_config on production collections only if the artifact meets them, else record non-adoption.
who: owner (thresholds + adoption call); backend storage (rollout if adopted)
where: pre-registered target sheet; production collection configs (only touched on a green artifact)
why: quantization trades quality for RAM — only the owner can price that trade
feasibility_note: 'Agreed thresholds' do not exist yet anywhere in the plan or registries; per the hypothesis-promotion standing feedback they must be numeric and set in advance, or the experiment's conclusion will be negotiated after the fact. The Experiment tag already forbids adoption without before/after evaluation — this row is the decision seat for it.
required_receipt: Signed threshold sheet dated before the eval run + the adoption/non-adoption decision referencing the L1174 artifact.

verbatim: Benchmark current warmed MPS reranker at realistic candidate shapes.
action: Benchmark the DEPLOYED reranker (:8081 — verify first WHICH binary serves it: torch fp16 MPS sidecar per current deployment, reranker_mlx is rollback) after S0's warmup merge, at realistic candidate shapes taken from live diagnostics (k x candidate-length distributions from the P1.9 latency-by-shape data), recording embedder co-load state during every run.
where: :8081 sidecar (plist-managed torch fp16 / rollback reranker_mlx); shape distributions from retrieval diagnostics; benchmark artifact under docs/baselines
why: an unwarmed or wrong-binary benchmark would misprice the serving alternatives comparison
feasibility_note: TERMINOLOGY (registry_leftover 'reranker readiness' + user memory): THREE implementations answer to 'the reranker' — the deployed torch fp16 MPS sidecar on :8081, the reranker_mlx rollback backend, and a repo /reranker docker service with NO warmup; 'current warmed MPS reranker' must name the :8081 binary explicitly (kickstart-env/plist drift is a known gotcha). 'Warmed' depends on P1.8 warmup code that is IN CODE pending S0's merge — S-order handles it. Metal contention rule (memory): embedder and reranker share ONE Metal GPU; the benchmark must control/record embedder load or p95 is meaningless.
required_receipt: Benchmark artifact: binary identity (endpoint /health or process check) + warm-state proof + throughput/p50/p95 across >=3 realistic shape buckets, embedder-idle and embedder-busy variants.

verbatim: Benchmark CPU/int8 ONNX only if the selected model exports correctly.
action: Attempt ONNX export of the selected cross-encoder first (export + parity check on a fixed query-doc set within tolerance); only if parity passes, benchmark CPU int8 ONNX under the same shape buckets as L1180; if export fails or drifts, record that and skip.
where: offline export environment (scratch); same benchmark harness as L1180; parity fixture set
why: a CPU-int8 path only matters if the exact production model exports faithfully — the guard is the point
feasibility_note: The conditional is correctly written (benchmark ONLY if export is correct) — it already encodes the guardrail. Risk is ordinary ML-ops friction (opset/tokenizer mismatches); parity must be scored against the fp16 sidecar's scores, not just rank order, because the calibrated sigma((s-mu)/T) calibration (memory) rides absolute scores.
required_receipt: Export parity artifact (score deltas within stated tolerance on the fixture set) then benchmark table, OR a recorded export-failure note closing the box as skipped-by-guard.

verbatim: Compare throughput, p50/p95, ranking quality, memory, and contention.
action: Produce the comparison matrix across serving options: throughput, p50/p95 by shape bucket, ranking quality (rank correlation + heldout nDCG delta vs the deployed sidecar), memory footprint, and Metal/CPU contention (embedder-busy deltas); one table, same fixtures, same hardware.
where: harness from L1180/L1181; heldout subset for quality; docker/host stats for memory
why: the serving decision is multi-axis — a single-axis win (speed) is exactly what L1183 forbids acting on
feasibility_note: All inputs exist by construction of the two prior rows. Quality axis must be measured, not assumed invariant across runtimes: int8 + different kernels can shift calibrated scores enough to reorder marginal candidates, which the answerability gates downstream are sensitive to. Contention axis is mandated by the Metal-GPU memory (never assume the sidecars are isolated).
required_receipt: Single comparison artifact with all five axes for every candidate runtime, fixtures and hardware pinned, dated.

verbatim: Do not switch models or runtimes based on theoretical speed alone.
action: Keep as the adoption gate: any model/runtime switch requires the L1182 matrix green on quality and contention axes plus an owner sign-off; theoretical or vendor-benchmark speed claims are inadmissible as evidence.
where: P3.5 adoption decision; deploy path for the :8081 sidecar (plist/LaunchAgent — remember apple-ml sidecars deploy from a COPY, repo edits need re-install)
why: the reranker is the sole scoring authority under the production redesign — a mis-switched runtime degrades every answer
feasibility_note: Standing guardrail matching two adopted feedbacks: think-before-executing (predict outcome before model swaps) and hypothesis-promotion (numeric targets in advance). History justifies it: the previous MLX 'cross-encoder' was a bi-encoder cosine sham (memory) — precisely the failure a theoretical-speed switch invites. Deployment gotcha if adopted: sidecars run from a rsynced COPY (~/PolymathRuntime), so a runtime switch needs re-install, not git pull.
required_receipt: Absence of any runtime/model switch commit without a linked L1182 artifact + owner sign-off; if a switch ships, its deploy includes the re-install receipt and a live /health binary check.
